coredata.dc:title: Gold versus platinum for chemical modification of carbon quantum dots from carboxymethyl cellulose: Tunable biomedical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Anazi, Menier
coredata.dc:description: Urgent requirements for medication from chronic inflammation and cancer are considerably interested, while, the recent reports were considered with investigating simple methods for synthesis. Metal-modified carbon quantum dots (“M-CQDs”) were successfully ingrained from carboxymethyl cellulose under the assistance of infra-red irradiation. The current approach demonstrates a study for the effect of structural tuning for biomedical performance of CQDs via modifying of CQDs with either gold (Au-CQDs) or platinum (Pt-CQDs). Successive nucleation of Au-CQDs and Pt-CQDs was confirmed via different instrumental analyses like, TEM micrographs, Zeta potential, XRD, FTIR, 1HNMR& 13CNMR spectra. The data reveal that, modification of CQDs (8.7 nm) with gold was reflected in insignificant effect on the mean size of CQDs (8.9 nm), whereas, doping of platinum resulted in slight enlargement of the size (12.4 nm). However, Pt-CQDs were exhibited with the highest anti-inflammatory (cell viability percent 78 %) and antimicrobial action. On the other hand, Au-CQDs were shown with the highest anticancer affinity (reduction of cell viability 83 %) compared to the others. The current study approved the superiority of CQDs modified with either gold or platinum to be successfully applicable as potential therapeutic reagents for the treatment of either cancer or inflammation diseases.

head.ce:title: Gold versus platinum for chemical modification of carbon quantum dots from carboxymethyl cellulose: Tunable biomedical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Tabuk, Tabuk 71491, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Tabuk, Tabuk 71491, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Urgent requirements for medication from chronic inflammation and cancer are considerably interested, while, the recent reports were considered with investigating simple methods for synthesis. Metal-modified carbon quantum dots (“M-CQDs”) were successfully ingrained from carboxymethyl cellulose under the assistance of infra-red irradiation. The current approach demonstrates a study for the effect of structural tuning for biomedical performance of CQDs via modifying of CQDs with either gold (Au-CQDs) or platinum (Pt-CQDs). Successive nucleation of Au-CQDs and Pt-CQDs was confirmed via different instrumental analyses like, TEM micrographs, Zeta potential, XRD, FTIR, 1HNMR& 13CNMR spectra. The data reveal that, modification of CQDs (8.7 nm) with gold was reflected in insignificant effect on the mean size of CQDs (8.9 nm), whereas, doping of platinum resulted in slight enlargement of the size (12.4 nm). However, Pt-CQDs were exhibited with the highest anti-inflammatory (cell viability percent 78 %) and antimicrobial action. On the other hand, Au-CQDs were shown with the highest anticancer affinity (reduction of cell viability 83 %) compared to the others. The current study approved the superiority of CQDs modified with either gold or platinum to be successfully applicable as potential therapeutic reagents for the treatment of either cancer or inflammation diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: “Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)” as optically active nano-objects were also identified as carbon dots (CDs) and graphene dots (GDs), have extensively attracted the attention, whereas, they were accidentally investigated in literature [1]. Generally, CQDs are expressed to be characterized with several preferable properties like chemical inactivity, stability against photo-bleaching, interchangeable photoluminescence emission, simplicity of synthesis, low cytotoxic effects and biocompatibility [2–4]. Therefore, CQDs show variety in applications for “bio-imaging, energy conversion and storing, catalysis, drug carriers, environmental purposes and remediation” [5–9]. Till now, surface functionalization and limited quantum yield (QY) are ascribed as two disadvantages that reflected in the applicability of CQDs [10]. In the recent approaches that considered with the mechanism of optical activity for CQDs reported that; the surface decoration and doping of heteroatoms within the main skeleton are two effective pathways to enhance the optical activity and widen the scope of applications for CQDs [11–13]. However, surface decoration via ingraining from different polymeric precursors and organic reagents involves complex synthetic and ultra-filtration steps with a limited quantum yield and diminished some of the original functional centers for specific analytical and sensing applications [14–16]. Also, it was reported that; doping of heteroatoms within the main skeleton is relatively convenient for the wide-scaled preparation, focused on changing the electronic compositions related to CQDs energy gaps, leads to endow CQDs with various intrinsic characters via the introduction of non-metal moieties and metallic moieties into CQDs. Some approaches were reported that; CQDs are characterized with electronic configuration and exhibited with σ/σ*, σ/Π*, Π/Π*, n/Π*, and n/σ*, that are responsible for their physicochemical characters, especially their luminescent characters (absorption and photoluminescence) [11,17]. It is thought that, heteroatoms that are chemically interacted with CQDs could affect the reaction between Π- and n-states in CQDs by the orbital overlapping and heteroatom nature as electron withdrawal/donation moieties. The available synthetic techniques for metal-modified CQDs or “M-CQDs” focus on the reaction between the metallic compounds and the organic moieties under special experimental conditions, that can be categorized to two techniques of “one-step” and “multi-step” synthetic techniques. “One-step” synthetic methodologies like; “hydrothermal, pyrolysis, solvo-thermal, microwave-assisted and ultrasonic chemical methods”. Additionally, “M-CQDs” could also be synthesized via “multi-step” pathways, by using of enlarged metal ions-doped carbonic resources [18–22].

Hydrothermal technique is the most applicable pathway for synthesis of “M-CQDs” owing to its convenient ingraining, cheapness, high effective and environmentally safe. This methodology is ascribed to be proceeded via the treatment of the mixture from the small organic moieties such as “citric acid and ascorbic acid” with metal doping at high temperature for certain period of time. Till now, metal-doped CQDs “M-CQDs” such as K-GQDs “potassium-doped GQDs” [23], Cu-CDs “copper-doped CDs” [24,25], Zn-CDs “zinc-doped CDs” [26] and two types of heteroatoms co-doped CQDs like Mg, N-CDs “magnesium and nitrogen co-doped CDs” [27,28] were synthesized with this technique. “Tang and Meng's groups” preliminary succeeded in the hydrothermal synthesis of Cu-CDs with freshly mixed “Cu (NO3)2 and sodium salt of poly-methacrylic acid” at 240 °C for 9 h [24]. Cu-CDs prepared from “sodium citrate and corpus chloride” as precursors with 1: 0.25 of “sodium citrate: copper chloride” to be at 200 °C for 6 h were also prepared in recent study [25]. In more recent study, Ma et al. were prepared Cu, N-CDs from “citric acid monohydrate, copper acetate monohydrate and diethylene triamine” under the hydrothermal conditions at 230 °C for 12 h [29]. In another typical method, CA, Mg (OH)2 and ethylene diamine were dissolved in ultra-purified water while the reaction liquor was heated at 200 °C for 3 h to synthesize Mg, N-CQDs [27,28]. The obtained colloids were centrifuged at “12,000 rpm for 30 minutes” and dialyzed to obtain the required hydrophilic Mg, N-CQDs (2–8 nm). HRTEM “High-resolution transmission electron microscopy” images affirmed the nucleation of crystalline Mg, N-CDs with “lattice spacing of 0.23 and 0.25 nm" that is closer to graphite's facet.

Obeying to the green chemistry aspects, it is a considerable challenge for nucleating CQDs from biopolymer with anticancer and anti-inflammatory potency. In recent years, different reports were considerably interested in studying the biomedical potentiality of CQDs. Recent approaches were oriented the researchers toward investigating the biomedical applications of CQDs, relevant to the chronic diseases. Urgent requirements for medication from cancer and chronic inflammation are considerably interested, as the recent reports were considered with the investigation of simple/cost less techniques for synthesizing of CQDs to be successfully applicable as effective therapeutics. Herein, for the first time, M-modified CQDs were successfully ingrained from carboxymethyl cellulose under the assessment of infra-red irradiation. Currently, a systematic approach demonstrates a study for the effect of structural tuning via modifying of CQDs with either gold or platinum to prepare Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs as biomedically active CQDs. Whereas, carboxy methyl cellulose (CMC) was applied as sprout for clustering of the required QDs. Successive nucleation of either Au-CQDs or Pt-CQDs (both are referred as metal-modified CQDs; “M-CQDs”) was affirmed via UV–Visible spectroscopy, TEM micrographs, XRD, FTIR, 1HNMR& 13CNMR spectra. The antitumor action of Au-CQDs versus Pt-CQDs was also investigated. A comparable overview for following up the anti-inflammation performance of the prepared QDs was represented, in order to affirm their supremacy for inhibiting the inflammation. In addition to a study for their antimicrobial performance was also demonstrated.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, sodium alt, low viscosity, 50–200 cP, 4 % in H2O at 25 °C, Sigma-Aldrich), gold chloride (HAuCl4.3H2O, ≥99.9 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and platinum chloride (PtCl2, 98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98 %, pellets, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied as received, whereas, the stock solutions were prepared form deionized H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CMC for clustering of CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g/L of CMC was preliminary dissolved within 40 g/L of sodium hydroxide under magnetic stirring for 1 h at 90 °C, while, metal modification is consequently performed via mixing with 1mmole/L of either gold chloride or platinum chloride. Afterward, under infra-red assisted conditions, complete clustering of “M-CQDs” (Au-CQDs or Pt-CQDs) is proceeded, whereas, the prepared nano-colloids were separately transferred to autoclave cup to be kept under the effect of infra-red irradiation at 135 °C. Eventually, the reaction liquors with reddish brown color were cooled at room temperature, for sequential dialysis with distilled water for ultra-filtration to get highly purified/monodispersed CQDs. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram for clarification of the successive exploitation of CMC for clustering the desirable “M-CQDs”.

Absorption spectra were analyzed at 250–750 nm via spectrophotometer “Cary 100 UV-VIS, UV–Vis-NIR Systems, from Agilent”, and the data were collected at “500 – 4000 cm–1 using 15 points smoothing, 4 cm–1 resolution, 64 scanning times with scanning rate of 2 mm.sec-1”. Topographical and size distribution of the synthesized CQDs & “M-CQDs” were identified via anticipation of “HRTEM, JEOL-JEM-1200, from Japan”. Size distribution of CQDs & “M-CQDs” was estimated using “4 pi analysis software, from USA”. Infrared spectra were analyzed via “Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer”. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectral maps were obtained from “Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometry, JEOL – Japan”. Photoluminescence spectra for the prepared QDs were collected using “JASCO FP8300 fluorometer “, with excitation at 340 nm, whereas, the results were collected at ambient conditions, while, the emission spectra were detected. Zeta potential for the prepared CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs was estimated via “Zetasizer analyzer with dynamic light scattering technique at 25 °C in insulated chamber (Malvern Nano ZS, from Malvern Instruments Ltd – UK)”. All of the prepared QDs were analyzed via “powder X-ray diffraction, X'Pert MPD diffractometer system from Philips, at room temperature”, as the diffraction bands were estimated in a diffraction angle (2θ) of 3.5–50° “using monochromator, Cu Kα X-radiation at 40 kV, 50 mA and λ = 1.5406 Å".X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy “XPS X-Ray – FG ON 400 μm" was exploited for analyzing the prepared QDs, whereas, the analyzed samples were neutralized via “low energy electrons (0.1 eV), with hybrid mode (with electrostatic and magnetic lenses), 10-22 scanning and CAE 50 were used and the excitation of photoelectrons by monochromatic Alpha irradiation”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell proliferative assay and antitumor performance

ce:section.ce:para: A 431 as human epidermoid skin cancer cells were given from “Nawah Scientific Inc., Mokatam, Cairo, Egypt”, whereas, the cells maintained in DMEM medium that were supplemented with “100mg/mL of streptomycin, 100units/mL of penicillin and 10% of heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum in humidified, 5% (v/v) CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C".

the cell viability percent was estimated via “SRB assay” [30,31]. In this procedure, 100 μL aliquot of cell suspension (5 × 10^3cells) was dropped in 96-well plates to be incubated for one day. The cells were treated with additional aliquot of CDs (100 μL) with different concentrations. Afterwards, the cells were maintained via the replacement of the medium with 150 μL of 10 % TCA to be incubated at 4 °C for 60 min. TCA was removed, and the prepared cells were washed for five times with distilled H2O. However, aliquots of 70 μL SRB solution (0.4%w/v) were dropped to incubate the plates in dark at ambient conditions for 10 min. Eventually, the prepared plates were washed for three times with 1 % acetic acid for overnight air-drying, then, 150 μL of TRIS (10 mM) was exploited for dissolving the protein-bonded SRB stain, whereas, the absorption was detected at 540 nm via Infinite F50 microplate readers (TECAN, Switzerland).

For approving the anti-inflammatory performance of the as-prepared QDs, “RAW264.7 cells, Murine macrophages, ATCC®” were allowed for growing in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Media, DMEM, Corning, USA“ for supplement with 10% of “fetal bovine serum, 100 U/mL of penicillin, 100 μg/mL of streptomycin sulphate and 2 mM of l-glutamine in humidified incubator with 5 % of carbon dioxide“. Formerly, the cells were washed using “buffered phosphate saline“ with sterilization, then the glassy reactors were scrapped with “SPL, Spain“. Aliquots of “RAW 264.7 cells, 0.5 × 106 cells/mL“ were dropped in “96-well plates“ for 12 hours. Subsequently, “non-induced triplicate wells“ were added in media with samples vehicle, “DMSO, 0.1 % v/v”. Whereas, the inflammation inducer with “lipopolysaccharide (LPS) as 100 ng/mL“ was dropped to inflammatory groups of the triplicate wells for completing the culture media, containing “0.1 % v/v of dimethyl sulfoxide”. The samples were treated with screen concentration of 10 and100 μM of QDs that were dissolved in DMSO (0.1%, by volume) to be diluted in LPS. “0.25 mM of Indo (Indomethacin)” was examined as blank sample. After incubating for one day, SRB assay [30,31] was carried out for the estimation of cell viability percent. Whereas, aliquots of “100μL cell suspension (5x10^3cells) were added in 96-well plates” for incubation in complete medium for one day. Cells were modified with another reagent of “100μL media containing drugs with different concentrations”. After exposing to drug, the cells were fixed via replacing medium with “150 μL of 10% TCA to be incubated at 4°C for 60 minutes. Afterward, TCA was removed, whereas, the cells were washed for five times with the distilled water. Aliquot of “70μL SRB solution (0.4%w/v)“ was added to be sequentially incubated in dark at ambient conditions for 10 minutes. The plates were washed for three times with 1% acetic acid to be overnight air dried. Then, “150μL of TRIS (10 mM)” were added for dissolving the protein-bonded to the SRB stain moieties; the absorption was estimated at 540 nm via “Infinite F50 microplate reader” (TECAN, Switzerland) [31].

The antimicrobial potentiality of the prepared QDs against three microbial strains was qualitatively evaluated via “Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion method” [32,33]. In this procedure, three pathogens of “+ve gram bacterial strain (Bacillus cereus), –ve gram bacterial strain (Escherichia coli) and fungal strain (Candida albicans)” were selected, while, the pathogenic strains were grown to obtain the pathogenic suspension.100 μL of pathogenic suspension was dispersed onto agar plate to the broth in which it was fixed. 10 μL of QDs was dropped in the plates for incubation at 37 °C for one day. Inhibition zone diameter in millimeters was estimated via the slipped calipers as reported in NCCLS, 1997 [34].

The average size was estimated using 4 pi software for 50 particles, while the average and the standard deviation were considered. For the biomedical activity (antibacterial, anticancer and anti-inflammatory), the measurements were performed for three times and the mean values were collected with considering the error bars/standard deviations. All the presented Figures were drawn by Microsoft excel 2016.

The demonstrated study is focused on the exploitation of CMC as nucleator for “M-CQDs” with the assistance of infra-red irradiation (Fig. 1). The effect of metal precursor on the tunability of the optical characters and biomedical performance of the as-prepared CQDs, is the main goal of the current approach. Meanwhile, gold chloride and platinum chloride were exploited to dop gold and platinum metals for successive clustering of Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs, respectively.

The mechanism of “M-CQDs” clustering from alkali solubilized CMC could be hypothesized as follows; CMC macromolecules were supposed to be depolymerized under the strong alkaline conditions to smaller fragments, whereas, under the effect of infra-red irradiation, the as-produced fragments are aromatized, repolymerized and oxidized for clustering of controllable sized CQDs modified with the metallic moieties that are supposed to be trapped within the intermolecular spaces [35–38]. Eventually, the as-produced nano-colloids are dialyzed for ultra-filtration to give highly purified “M-CQDs” with controllable geometry [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of metal modified CQDs (Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Visible spectra for CQDs before and after metal modification were presented in Fig. 2a, whereas, it could be declared that, clustering of “M-CQDs” under the effect of IR-irradiation, resulted in the detection of all the examined QDs with sharp absorption peak at 210–225 nm with long tail in visible range, signing for “π - π* transition and electron radiative relaxation” [40–42]. This finding is in harmony with that reported in literature [6], whereas, the optical spectral maps of the all-prepared QDs are significantly observable in the ultraviolet light range.

The fluorescence emission was performed for the prepared CQDs before and after metal modification as shown in Fig. 2b. By excitation at wavelength of 370 nm, the prepared CQDs exhibited emission peak at 464 nm (greenish region) which is related to the surface decoration of CQDs with non-bonding & mobile pi- electrons of the decorative heteroatom containing groups [10,16,43]. After modification with metals (either Au or Pt), the intensity of fluorescence emission is reduced from 55 a.m.u to 41 and 32 a.m.u, respectively. This reflects the quenching effect of metal (Au and Pt) which carried out by the interaction with the decorative heteroatom groups in CQDs [44].

NMR spectral mapping was plotted in Figs. 3 and 4
, for prediction of the reaction mechanism and to approve the successive nucleation of “M-CQDs” from CMC under the effect of IR- irradiation. on the other hand, Fig. 3 shows 13CNMR spectral data of the above-examined QDs samples and it could be observably detected that, either with or without metal modification, the typical bands for CQDs ingrained from CMC were detected at 16.99–23.51 ppm, 31.14–34.29 ppm, 57.53–61.36 ppm, 68.59–72.18 ppm, 167.91–171.27 ppm and 181.42–183.04 ppm, that are corresponding to “CH2 (sp3), CH (sp3), -C(sp3), carbon attached to hydroxyl group, C=C aromatic or sp2 and C=O”, respectively. Fig. 4 shows 1HNMR for QDs prepared from CMC with or without metal mixing under the effect of IR irradiation. The spectral data show that, all of the examined QDs were characterized with significant bands at 1.19–2.27 ppm, 3.81–3.99 ppm, 4.68–4.99 ppm, 5.91 ppm and 8.30–8.33 ppm, referring to protons of “sp3 C-H, hydroxyl groups, attached to carbonyl groups, O-C-H alcohols, aromatic or sp2 CH=CH”, respectively. From the explained spectra, it could be depicted that, either with or without metal modification, all of the examined QDs were shown with characteristic peaks of CH aromatic and CH (aldehyde), to affirm the elucidated mechanism of clustering CQDs via aromatization and repolymerization of CMC fragments under the effect of infra-red irradiation. Whereas, the detection of characteristic peaks for O-C-H alcohols and CH aldehyde, revealed that, alkali dissolved CMC is compatible for producing of O-surface passivated CQDs, as elucidated in the mechanism of nucleation.

Fig. 5a represents FT-IR spectra for CQDs before and after their modification with either gold (Au-CQDs) or platinum (Pt-CQDs), in order to show the effect of metal modification on the chemical compositions and the surface passivation of the nucleated QDs. Fig. 5a shows the significant peaks of CMC at “3323 cm−1, 2946 cm−1, 1611 cm−1, 1419 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1”, signing for “O-H stretching, interlayer C-H (sp3) stretch, C=O stretch, CH (Sp3) bend, and alkoxy C-O", respectively. Whereas, after its exploitation for nucleation of CQDs, all the as-mentioned bands were persisted in addition to new peaks for “C=C stretch (aromatic) at 1522-1473 cm-1 and aromatic sp2 CH bend at 894 cm-1 “ are observably detected, that are significant for the aromatic nature of CQDs [45,46]. However, metal-modified CQDs were shown with more additional peak at 656 cm−1, corresponding to O-M bond to affirm the successive doping of either Au or Pt via coordinative bonding with O-containing decorative groups of the ingrained CQDs to give the desirable QDs, Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs, respectively.

On the other hand, Fig. 5b shows Zeta-potential data for CQDs before and after metal modification. Whereas, zeta-potential is a key factor for describing the degree of electrostatic repulsion between similar charged particles in a colloidal system. The negative value of zeta-potential assigned for the repulsive electrostatic forces between the dispersed particles in the colloidal liquor, i.e., larger is the negative value, higher is the repulsion between colloidal particles, i.e., more stabilized colloidal system [42,47]. In accordance to literature, smaller valued zeta potential is attributed to the particle coagulation as a result of weak attraction forces (van der Waals forces), leading to physical non-stabilization [48,49]. From the presented data in Fig. 5b, it could be identified that, CQDs colloid that was synthesized with alkaline hydrolysis of CMC under the effect of infra-red irradiation, was exhibited with the highest estimated negative value of zeta-potential (−21.9 mV), whereas, an observable diminishing in the estimated value was detected after metal modification, to be −2.8 mV & -5.3 mV, for Au-CQDs and Pt-CQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, modification with gold could undesirably diminish the stability of CQDs rather that for platinum. So, it could be decided that, doping of gold could be more preferable for nucleation of higher stabilized “M-CQDs”.

Fig. 6 represents TEM microscopic images of CQDs either before or after metal modification that were successively ingrained from CMC under the effect of infra-red irradiation. The plotted images show the topography and geometrical features of the synthesized QDs, with the estimation of size average. All of the represented micrographs were significantly approved the affinity and accessibility of CMC as QDs nucleator under the effect of infra-red irradiation, for successive nucleation of size and geometry controlled-QDs, in addition to follow up the effect of metal modification for clustering of spherical/highly stable QDs.

The particle size and size distribution of the synthesized dots were estimated from the micro-images in Fig. 6 and from the Zetasizer data. The estimated particle size from TEM images was slightly smaller than that measured from Zetasizer. From the estimated data of size average (Fig. 7 ), it could be identified that, the exploitation of alkali-hydrolyzed CMC under the effect of infra-red irradiation, resulted in clustering of CQDs with particle size of 8.7 nm (6.7 nm from TEM, Fig. 7a). Whereas, the modification with gold salt is not affected on the particle size (Au-CQDs, mean size 8.9 nm, 8.2 nm from TEM, Fig. 7b), in contrast to the chemical modification with platinum salt, that is reflected in sensible size enlargement of 12.4 nm (Pt-CQDs, 11.6 nm from TEM, Fig. 7c).

Fig. 8 shows the analyzed data of XRD that were estimated for either CQDs or “M-CQDs” that were clustered from CMC under the effect of IR-radiation, to declare that, CMC is shown with two peaks at 2θ = 12.7° & 19.8°. CQDs were exhibited with six diffraction bands at 2θ = 28.9°, 32.2°, 33.6° & 37.8°, whereas, metal modification of CQDs resulted in estimation of new peaks at 2θ = 38.1° & 44.5°, which are significant for the metallic Au, in case of Au-CQDs. While, in case of Pt, new peaks at 2θ = 45.5° & 66.6° that are corresponding to the metallic Pt. This approves the successive crystallization of metallic Au and Pt within CQDs matrix.

XPS data were shown for CQDs either with or without metal modification in Fig. 9 , whereas, the data could also give further approval for the chemical structure and successive modification of CQDs with either gold or platinum. The survey of spectra could reveal that, binding energy of “C1s, O1s, Au4f and Pt4f”, were assigned at 281–291, 529–538, 81–92 and 69–80 eV, respectively [50]. “C1s bands” were shown at binding energy of 284.7/284.9, 285.9/286.5 and 288.9/287.8 eV that were signified for CC, CH and CO, respectively [51]. O1s, bands of binding energy were estimated at 530.9/531.5, 532.8/533.1 and 535.5/534.4 eV, signed for “C-O, C-O-O and O-M", respectively. In case of Au4f, binding energy peaks were estimated at 83.9 and 87.7/88.6 eV, assigning for 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of the metallic form of metallic gold (Au0). While, in case of platinum, binding energy was detected at 72.3 and 75.9 eV, referring to 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of the metallic form of platinum (Pt0). These are clearly approved the successive anchoring of either metallic Au or Pt within CQDs matrix [52–55]. According to literature [55–57], the bands at 88.6 & 75.9 eV, corresponding to the 4f7/2 of AuO & PtO, confirm the successive chemical modification of CQDs via coordinative bonds with O-containing decorative groups of CQDs, that is in harmony with the above-explained spectral maps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammation performance of metal modified CQDs; Au-CQDs versus Pt-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
One of the harmful impact of inflammation is proteins decomposition [58]. The decomposed protein act in significant motivation of immune cells at the sites of inflammation, leading to dramatical changing of “acute inflammation” to “chronic inflammation” [58,59]. Progress in the growth rate of the cancer cells, i.e., “Tumorigenesis”, is also reported to be a result of “chronic inflammation” [60]. The transformation of “acute inflammation” to “chronic inflammation” occurs when controllability of acute inflammation is non-efficiently proceeded and clearance of inflammation mediator is failed [61]. Consequently, the inflammation mediator content continuously raises, leading to the liberation of RS such as; “O2
. , OH . , 1O2, and H2O2” and RNS “nitrogen reactive species; such as “NO . , ONOO-” that are responsible for the denaturation of nucleic acids [61,62]. The liberated ROS & RNS acts in oxidizing the intermolecular ingredients and decomposition of genes and proteins, leads to the transformation of “acute inflammation” to “chronic inflammation” [63]. So, it could be declared that, the action of anti-inflammatory reagents is mainly maintained in acceleration sweeping of ROS and RNS for slowing down such type of transformation.

In the represented approach, the SBR assay was proceeded to confirm the accessibility of the prepared metal-modified CQDs; either Au-CQDs or Pt-CQDs, for anti-inflammation action, and for variation of the metal modification on the anti-inflammation performance. Herein, the anti-inflammation action was followed up via inhibiting the liberation of “nitrous oxide radicals; NO . ” as one of RNS that are responsible for transformation of “acute inflammation’ to “chronic inflammation”, to increase the cell viability. Meanwhile, the higher anti-inflammatory action, the higher is cell viability % (i.e., increase in inhibition percent of “nitrous oxide radicals” resulted in enhancement of the cell viability). Fig. 10a shows the relation between cell viability and the concentration of the synthesized QDs to declare that, regardless to the metal modification effect, increment of QDs concentration from 1 μg/L up to 1000 μg/L, leading to the significant increment in cell viability percentage, i.e., all of the prepared QDs were shown with efficient anti-inflammation action. However, modification of CQDs with platinum resulted in the highest anti-inflammation action, as the cell viability percent was significantly increased from 5 %, 6 % & 4 % using 1 μg/L to 45 %, 60 % & 78 %, using 1000 μg/L of CQDs, Au-CQDs and Pt-CQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, increment of Pt-CQDs up to 1000 μg/mL resulted in significant maximizing of the cell viability percentage up to 78 %. The obtained anti-inflammatory activity for Pt-CQDs was almost similar to that of CQDs synthesized from the hetero compounds [10]. It could be decided that; Pt-CQDs exhibited with high affinity to be applicable as anti-inflammation laborer, i.e., as a potential therapeutic reagent for the treatment of inflammation, production of vaccine, immune-therapeutic reagent, and immune-suppressed drug.

The mechanism of anti-inflammation action of QDs can be predicted in accordance to literature as the followings; QDs act in sweeping of RNS via the delocalizing electrons with their conjugative nuclei, so as CQDs could lower the oxidizing stress of such reactive species for slowing down their effects in the transformation of “acute” to “chronic inflammation”. Additionally, the currently prepared CQDs with metal doping could provoke low immune response [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs versus “M-CQDs” for anticancer potentiality via Necrosis
ce:section.ce:para: The accessibility of any anticancer drug for inhibiting the reproduction of alive tumor cells is known as cytotoxic effects, whereas, such drugs were assumed to act in cell mortality via the motivation of programmed cell death, or necrosis (i.e., apoptosis). In accordance to literature [65], necrosis is ascribed as the most favorable mechanism of action for the antitumor drugs. Recent studies were considerably interested in the investigation of the potency for different drugs that can act via motivating necrosis for the tumor cells. Moreover, activating of caspases (caspase 3 & caspase 9) is ascribed as the preliminary action for motivation and progression of the programmed cell death. Caspases are known as endoproteases, that principally act in the regulation of necrosis signal networking, via starting of the cellular decomposition, i.e., fragmentation of cyto-proteins or DNA [66].

In accordance to literature [65,67], the anticancer performance of the nucleated QDs in the current study could be elucidated as follows; penetration of QDs via the examined cell wall results in the activation of caspases, as the as-synthesized QDs in cytoplasm of the examined cells could liberate RS, that could efficiently activate the caspases to initiate the programmed cell death.

Fig. 9b represents the relationship between the estimated values for reduction of the cell viability percent and the concentration of CQDs, whereas, the increment in the estimated value for the reduction cell viability percent, meaning that, less is the viable cancer cells. The figured-out data obviously depict that; regardless to the metal modification of QDs, the estimated value of reduction for the cell viability percent was significantly diminished via increasing of QDs concentration up to1000 μg/mL. Whereas, the antitumor performance of the tested QDs follows the order of Au-CQDs > Pt-CQDs > CQDs. Meanwhile, modification of CQDs with gold was shown with the highest necrotic effects against the examined cancer cells, however, unmodified CQDs were exhibited with the lowest anti-cancer action. Moreover, the reduction of cell viability percentage was significantly increased from 18 %, 20 % & 24 %, using 10 μg/mL, to 57 %, 83 % & 75 %, using 1000 μg/mL, for CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs, respectively. The obtained anticancer activity for Au-CQDs is rationally higher than that of CQDs synthesized from microcrystalline cellulose, modified starch and modified chitosan [4,16,68], while is quite close to that of CQDs obtained from the hetero compounds [10]. It could be decided that; modification of CQDs with gold and platinum resulted in maximizing the anti-proliferative action against the examined cancer cells. In summarization, the metal modification of CQDs with either gold or platinum resulted in the enhancement of the cytotoxic effects against the tested cancer cells, and doping of gold with the matrix of CQDs produced more efficient active anti-proliferative drugs. Meanwhile, the results affirmed the seniority “M-CQDs” to be exploitable as antitumor drugs.

Numerous reports were considered with the exploitation of CQDs as antimicrobial reagents against different bacterial and fungal strains. In agreement with literature [69–76], the antimicrobial mechanism of action for the as-synthesized QDs could be described as follows; under the visible radiation in aqueous media, CQDs could generate RS, “such as singlet oxygen & hydroxyl free radicals”. Meanwhile, RS were assumed to penetrate the pathogenic cell wall to induce the oxidative stressing for the fragmentation of nucleic acids, leading to inhibit and alter the gene expression. Moreover, RS could act in dysfunction of mitochondria, peroxidation of lipids, inactivation of proteins, sequential decomposition of cell wall, followed by “apoptosis/necrosis” and cell death.

In the current study, the anti-pathogenic potentiality for QDs was evaluated against “positive gram bacterial species; B. cereus”, “negative-gram bacterial species; E. coli” and “fungal species; C. albicans’. The data in Table 1 significantly reveal that, against the tested microbial strains, either metal-modified and unmodified CQDs were shown with excellent antimicrobial potentiality. However, modification with platinum reflected in exhibition of CQDs with the highest antimicrobial action. Whereas, the reduction percent for the viability of microbial cells follows the order of Pt-CQDs > Au-CQDs > CQDs, against all the examined strains. Moreover, the reduction percentage for B. cereus was estimated to be 72.5 %, 91.5 % & 94.5 % for CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt -CQDs, respectively. The reduction percentage for E. coli was estimated to be 64.8 %, 83.7 % & 88.6 % for CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt -CQDs, respectively. However, the reduction percent for C. albicans was estimated to be 70 %, 89.8 % & 91 % for CQDs, Au-CQDs & Pt -CQDs, respectively. The antimicrobial activity of the M-CQDs is assumed to be much better than that of CQDs nucleated from microcrystalline cellulose, cyclodextrin and hetero compounds [10,44,68,77,78].

Meanwhile, metal modification of CQDs were shown to significantly enhance the antimicrobial potentiality owing to the exhibition of “M-CQDs” with more of size regulation for easier penetration through the microbial cell membrane to act in eventual cell mortality. The obtained data are in harmony with that in literature [69,72,74,75,79]. While, the accessibility of CQDs as potent anti-pathogenic reagents is essentially attributed to their composition with functional groups that act in pathogenic cell death via liberation of RS. Compared to the biologically active nano-metals and MOF (metal organic framework) which were recently reported in numerous studies [33,80–82], the as-prepared QDs were exhibited with significantly higher antimicrobial action. This could reflect the superior biocidal performance of the synthesized QDs, in addition to its low toxic effects, biocompatibility and cheapness. Therefore, it could be favorably exploited as antimicrobial reagents rather than nano-metals for biological applications. Moreover, the modification of CQDs with either Au or Pt is accompanied with higher antimicrobial activity rather than unmodified CQDs [42,68,77,83–85].

Recently, numerous studies were extensively considered with the investigation of the biological action and the optical activity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Recent approaches were focused on the exploitation of CQDs in the biomedical purposes, relevant to the chronic disease. Urgent requirements for medication from chronic inflammation and cancer are considerably interested, while, the recent reports were considered with study the easy methods for synthesis. Herein, for the first time, M-modified CQDs were successfully ingrained under the effect of infra-red irradiation. Currently, a systematic study is demonstrated for studying the structural tuning of CQDs via modifying of CQDs with either gold or platinum to prepare Au-CQDs & Pt-CQDs to enhance their biomedical performance. Whereas, carboxy methyl cellulose (CMC) was currently applied as sprout for clustering of the required QDs. Successive nucleation of either Au-CQDs or Pt-CQDs (both are referred as metal-modified CQDs; “M-CQDs”) was confirmed via different instrumental analyses like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, TEM micrographs, XRD, FTIR, 1HNMR& 13CNMR spectra. The antitumor action of Au-CQDs versus Pt-CQDs was also investigated. A comparable overview for following up the anti-inflammation performance of the prepared QDs was represented, in order to affirm their supremacy for inhibiting the inflammation. In addition to, a study for their anticancer and antimicrobial performance was also demonstrated. The data revealed that, modification of CQDs with gold was reflected in insignificant effect on the mean size of the nucleated CQDs, whereas, doping of platinum resulted in slight enlargement of the size. However, Pt-CQDs were exhibited with the highest anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial action. On the other hand, Au-CQDs were shown with the highest anticancer affinity. The demonstrated study could approve the superiority of CQDs modified with either gold or platinum to be successfully applicable as potential therapeutic reagents for treatment of various cancer and inflammation diseases.

Menier Al-Anazi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
